title: Venice Arsenal, Italy
description:
The main gate of the old Arsenal was built 1460 in Venetian Renaissance style. A stone sculpture of the winged Lion of Venice stands above the entrance. Of the four lion sculptures in front of the gate, the two directly either side were looted from Piraeus in 1687. The lion on the left is known as the Piraeus Lion and is 3m high. It was sculpted around 360BC and is famous for two runic inscriptions on its shoulders and flanks. It is likely that these were carved by Scandinavians in the 11th century.

The statue at the top is of Santa Giustina, and there are eight staues flanking the entrance. Six of these are allegorical representations of Wealth, Guard, the goddess Bellona, Neptune, Mars and Justice: the other two are unknown.

title: Arsenale di Venezia
description: Arsenal ist der Name der Schiffswerft, des Zeughauses und der Flottenbasis der ehemaligen Republik Venedig. Der Bau des Arsenals wurde unter dem Dogen Ordelaf Falier ab 1104 begonnen. Das Grundstück bestand aus zwei sumpfigen Inseln im Stadtteil Castello. Diese Werft, die als größter Produktionsbetrieb Europas vor dem Zeitalter der Industrialisierung betrachtet werden kann, wurde zum Vorbild für andere Marinearsenale in Europa.

title: Porta di Terra - 29868
tags: orthographic venice italy portaditerra arsenal bindersenforcedcactus lion statue santagiustina
description: The southern “land entrance” of the Arsenal, Venice. The entrance was erected in 1460. Above the entrance is the iconic winged Lion of Venice, displaying a closed book; signifying the state at war. At the apex is a statue of Santa Giustina, placed after the victory of the Battle of Lepanto (1571).

title: Arsenale di Venezia
tags: venice veneto italy italia italian renaissance arsenale portamagna bellini
description:
Porta Magna - main gates built around 1460

The Arsenal's main gate, the Porta Magna, was built around 1460 and was one of the first works of Venetian Renaissance architecture. It was based on the Roman Arch of the Sergii, a triumphal arch in Pula in Istra, now in Croatia but then Venetian territory. It was perhaps built by Antonio Gambello from a design by Jacopo Bellini. Two lions taken from Greece situated beside it were added in 1687. One of the lions, known as the Piraeus Lion, has runic defacements carved in it by invading Scandinavian mercenaries during the 11th century.

title: arsinale di venezia
tags: venezia italy venice arsenal arsinaledivenezia venicebiennale
description:
I had passed through Venice around two years earlier on my way to Slovenia. While snacking on cichetti, I met this American couple who said that they come to Venice every year to attend the Venice Bienniale. 2021 was the postponement of the 2020 Venice Architecture Biennale. 

As I like architecture and knew that there was a high probability that I would return to the US, I took this opportunity to go to the Architecture Biennale. 

One of the exhibition spaces is the Venice Arsenal-- the old shipbuilding site- a really cool venue

description:
Italy, Venice, street musicians playing by the towers of “Arsenale” for the restaurant outdoor customers at Campo de Arsenale
The entrance to the “Arsenale” at Campo de Arsenale with the wooden bridge crossing the Rio dell' Arsenale
The Arsenale used to be originally Venice’s shipyard, founded in 1104. Nowadays it belongs to the Italian marine corps & not accessible to the public, the only exception is every two years during the “Biennale”, the oldest established forum for contemporary art.

The arsenal was surrounded by walls & towers like a fortress, to which there were only two entrances; the water gate, "Ingresso all'Acqua", which is flanked by two towers erected in 1574 & if necessary, could be closed off by a gate. Through this gate, shipping traffic was handled over the harbour basin into the Mediterranean.

The land gate, portal gate - "Ingresso di Terra". The Land Gate is located next to the Water Gate, which in its current form was built around 1460 in the manner of a triumphal arch to show that Venice is famous for its wealth & fortune.
The entrance stairs are flanked by 8 symbolic figures. The two giant ancient lions to the right & left of the stairs are loot pieces that Francesco Morosini brought from Greece to Venice in 1687. The left three-meter-high lion had been near Athens in Porto Leone for two thousand years.

The lagoon city hides many picturesque places offside the tourist roads. If the buildings would be straight, painted & well maintained, I think Venice without this patina, the gondolas & gondolieri, channels, little bridges & the at all-time busy Canal Grande; all these main ingredients altogether express the romantic charm of Venice.  

👉 One World one Dream,
🙏...Danke, Xièxie 谢谢, Thanks, Gracias, Merci, Grazie, Obrigado, Arigatô, Dhanyavad, Chokrane to you & over 
14  million visits in my photostream with countless motivating comments

title: The Venetian Arsenal / Arsenale di Venezia
tags: arsenal shipyards armories construction architecture canal venezia venice venise veneto italia italie italy europe patrimoine industrialheritage
description: The Venetian Arsenal (Italian: Arsenale di Venezia) is a complex of former shipyards and armories clustered together in the city of Venice in northern Italy. Owned by the state, the Arsenal was responsible for the bulk of the Venetian republic's naval power during the middle part of the second millennium AD. It was "one of the earliest large-scale industrial enterprises in history". en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venetian_Arsenal

description:
This summer I did a solo cycling tour across Europe through 12 countries over the course of 3 months. I began my adventure in Edinburgh, Scotland and finished in Florence, Italy cycling 8,816 km. During my trip I took 47,000 photos. I recently got through my initial sifting through my photos and I'm now ready to share some of my favourites. 

Venetian Arsenal, Venice, Italy.

title: Venise - L' Arsenal
description:
L'Arsenal de Venise est situé dans le quartier du Castello dont il occupe une grande partie.
L'Arsenal a été pendant des siècles le cœur de la puissance militaire et économique de Venise. En effet, c'est ici qu'étaient fabriquées les flottes militaires et marchandes de la Sérénissime. 
L'essentiel de l'histoire Vénitienne est tourné vers la mer. La mer a été la meilleure protection de Venise face aux invasions mais c'est aussi sur mer que sa puissance s'est affirmée.
C'est dès le début du XIIème siècle que Venise décide de se doter d'un premier Arsenal d'état servant essentiellement à la réparation des galères et à y entreposer les divers équipements nécessaires à la marine. L'activité de construction de bateaux, même si elle était déjà présente à l'arsenal, était encore essentiellement réalisée dans des chantiers privés.
 
C'est au XIVème siècle que l'Arsenal prend son essor avec un quadruplement de sa surface avant de connaître un nouvel agrandissement un siècle plus tard. Venise a créé un système très original de location de ses galères aux marchands, qui les utilisaient ainsi en temps de paix, mais pouvaient être rapidement mobilisées en temps de guerre.
L'Arsenal au plus haut de son activité a compté 16 000 ouvriers, répartis sur le site de 25 hectares. La production des navires a été extrêmement rationalisée, structurée comme de véritables chaînes de production des siècles avant la révolution industrielle. L'Arsenal pouvait ainsi fabriquer une galère par jour en cas de nécessité ! Les pièces des navires étaient pour beaucoup standard et interchangeables ce qui présentait des avantages. Ainsi, ces pièces standardisées se trouvaient facilement dans les différents ports de l'empire Vénitien. De plus, les navires étant identiques, ils avaient le même comportement en mer, les convois étaient donc homogènes, avec la même vitesse et le même comportement en cas de tempête par exemple. 
Le personnel de l'Arsenal avait droit à certains privilèges et leur fonction était prestigieuse. Il était logé à bas coût, et leur corps constituait la garde d'Honneur du palais des Doges lors de l'élection d'un nouveau Doge. 
Encore aujourd'hui en partie zone militaire, vous pouvez en voir l'extérieur mais aussi passer à l'intérieur de la zone en vaporetto pour en voir un peu plus.
Entourant ce portail vous trouverez aussi des statues de lion, rapportées d'orient lors des diverses guerres menées par la Sérénissime. 
Le plus grand d'entre eux, a été rapporté du Pirée, le port d'Athènes, lors de la guerre opposant Vénitiens et Turcs en 1687. C'est d'ailleurs lors de cette même bataille que le Parthénon a été fortement endommagé, les Vénitiens faisant exploser un dépôt de munitions que les Turcs avaient placé là. 
Ce même lion est par ailleurs gravé de curieuses inscription qui ont été identifiées comme des caractères runiques que des mercenaires scandinaves ont laissé au XIème siècle lorsque les Byzantins ont envoyé ces troupes mater une révolte Athénienne. 
Le second lion par sa taille, provient aussi d'Athènes alors que les deux derniers, ont été ramenés de Constantinople, lors de la conquête de la ville par les croisés lors de la 4ème croisade.

title: The Piraeus Lion
tags: venice veneto italy it 2014 castle face lion sculpture art
description: The Piraeus Lion (now at the Arsenale di Venezia's Porta Magna) was originally located in Piraeus, the harbour of Athens. It was looted by Venetian naval commander Francesco Morosini in 1687 as plunder taken in the Great Turkish War against the Ottoman Empire.  The statue was defaced some time in the 2nd half of the 11th cen. by Scandinavians mercenaries in the service of the Byzantine Emperor who carved two lengthy runic inscriptions into the shoulders and flanks of the lion. The runes are carved in the shape of an elaborate lindworm dragon-headed scroll, in much the same style as on runestones in Scandinavia. Venice 2014

title: Porta Magna of the Venetian Arsenal
tags: venice veneto italy it 2014 architecture bridge castle city clock flag reflection river tower wall
description: By the 15th century, Venice was known as the “queen of the seas” because it was the main trade link between Europe and Asia. The city’s strength was felt all around the world for three reasons: sailing ability, strong economic organization, and its ability to construct its own boats. Venice's arsenal, the Arsenale, founded in 1104 and rebuilt in the 15th and 16th century, was one of the world’s wonders at the time. It was a public shipyard that combined the small, private workshops that were producing a particular prefabricated ship part or other maritime implement, such as munitions, rope, and rigging. The Arsenal's main gate, the Porta Magna, was built around 1460 and was the first Classical revival structure built in Venice. Venice 2014

title: Bellona, goddess of war
tags: camera venice statue female 17thcentury helmet goddess sword gateway limestone marble knee renaissance 17th arsenale penso bellona cabianca c17th goddessofwar herowinner francescopenso
description:
Statue at the gateway to the Venice Arsenale by Francesco Penso called "Cabianca" (1665?-1737) who was born and died in that city.
Bellona, the " fire-eyed maid of smoky war",  is usually portrayed with a helmet, sword, spear, and torch.
Here this 17th century cam-girl has discarded her spear and torch to show her shapely knee to the security camera!

title: Venetian Arsenal
tags: venice arsenal hdr contrast black white statue gate
description:
Construction of the Arsenal began around 1104, during Venice's republican era. It became the largest industrial complex in Europe prior to the Industrial Revolution, spanning an area of about 45 ha (110 acres), or about fifteen percent of Venice. Surrounded by a 2 mi (3.2 km) rampart, laborers and shipbuilders regularly worked within the Arsenal, building ships that sailed from the city's port. With high walls shielding the Arsenal from public view and guards protecting its perimeter, different areas of the Arsenal each produced a particular prefabricated ship part or other maritime implement, such as munitions, rope, and rigging. These parts could then be assembled into a ship in as little as one day. An exclusive forest owned by the Arsenal navy, in the Montello hills area of Veneto, provided the Arsenal's wood supply.

The Arsenal produced the majority of Venice's maritime trading vessels, which generated much of the city's economic wealth and power, lasting until the fall of the republic to Napoleon's conquest of the area in 1797. It is located in the Castello district of Venice, and it is now owned by the state. [wikipedia]

title: Venice Arsenale
tags: city travel venice vacation italy holiday heritage history industry water rio canal italian europe italia culture eu unescoworldheritagesite unesco worldheritagesite venetian shipyard venezia castello europeanunion venetia waterway armoury historiccity citta arsenale southerneurope centrostorico veneto thelagoon worldheritagelist northernitaly navalbase italianheritage theveneto venetianlagoon italianculture italianhistory cittàdivenezia communedivenezia veneziaeuganea veniceanditslagoons
description:
The gates to the Arsenale in Venice.

The Arsenale is a complex of former shipyards and armouries which helped the Venetian Republic launch a vast fleet into the waters of the Mediterranean.

It was built at the start of the 12th century and covers 45 hectares, within which labourers and shipbuilders worked on the boats of the empire.

The Arsenale was operational until the Napoleonic conquest of Venice in 1797, though its walls still stands and water still flows through its gates into the lagoon.

title: Leone del Pireo
tags: miguel canon eos venezia mb 450d canonef24105f4lis miguelbi leonedelpireo
description:
Il Leone del Pireo è una delle quattro statue di leoni presenti all'esterno dell'Arsenale di Venezia, simboli di San Marco, santo patrono della città. La statua originariamente si trovava al Pireo, l'antico porto di Grecia. Fu portata a Venezia come bottino dal comandante navale Francesco Morosini nel 1687, durante le guerre della Lega Santa contro l'Impero Ottomano, quando i veneziani assediarono Atene. Copie della statua si trovano al Museo Archeologico del Pireo e al Museo Storico di Stoccolma.

Il leone era un monumento molto noto nel Pireo, si trovava lì dal I o II secolo. La sua notorietà era tale che gli italiani chiamavano il porto Porto Leone.[1]

L'animale è seduto, ha la gola cava e aveva il segno di un tubo (ora scomparso) sul retro, ciò fa pensare ad un suo utilizzo precedente come fontana.[2]

La statua, di marmo bianco e alta circa 3 metri, è particolarmente conosciuta per essere stata vandalizzata attorno alla seconda metà dell'XI secolo da alcuni scandinavi che incisero due lunghe iscrizioni runiche sulle spalle e sui fianchi del leone.[3] Le rune sono incise seguendo la forma di un elaborato lindworm (dragone), motivo ricorrente in altre pietre runiche che si trovano in Scandinavia.[4]

Gli incisori erano quasi certamente Variaghi, mercenari scandinavi al servizio dell'Impero Bizantino che erano stati inviati in Grecia a reprimere una rivolta della popolazione locale.

title: Lion by the Porta Magna, Venetian Arsenal | Venetian lions - 12
tags: venice blackandwhite bw italy monochrome italia 17thcentury lion venezia patronsaint shipyards stmark 15thcentury seventeenthcentury fifteenthcentury img2130 venetianarsenal portamagna
description:
Lion beside the Porta Magna of the Venetian Arsenal, a complex of shipyards and armouries. 

The Porta Magna was built around 1460 and was the first Classical revival structure built in Venice. Two lions taken from Greece situated beside it were added in 1687.

title: Delian lion
tags: venice italy sculpture greek head lion classical venise venezia venedig
description: 11.4.2010: Lion, 6th cent. BC, stolen by the Venetians from Delos in the 17th cent. and now guarding the entrance to the Arsenal, Venice. I was irritated to discover later that one of the other sculpted lions here has Viking runic inscriptions all over it left by Varangian mercenaries in the 11th century, and I didn't even notice them. Oh well, a good excuse for a trip back.

title: Arsenale - Venezia

description: La Torre di destra dell'Arsenale di Venezia Per l'Arsenale di Venezia non esiste una data di fondazione: la notizia che lo volle fondato ai primi del XII secolo, nel 1104, dal doge Ordelafo Faliero, per l'esigenza di dare maggiore sviluppo alla cantieristica, un'attività strategica per la Serenissima, è derivata da una falsa medaglia commemorativa realizzata nel XIX secolo.[senza fonte] L'ubicazione dell'area, compresa tra le zone conventuali di San Pietro di Castello e la parrocchia di San Giovanni in Bragora (Darsena Vecchia), fu decisa sia per motivi strategici (difesa contro eventuali attacchi nemici) che logistici (qui si trovava il punto di arrivo del legname proveniente dal Cadore). Il primo nucleo, documentabile fin dagli inizi del XIII secolo, è costituito da due file di squeri ai lati della Darsena Vecchia. Vi si può accedere dal Bacino di San Marco solo attraverso uno stretto canale. All'inizio del Trecento, in seguito alle aumentate esigenze navali della città, fu aggiunto il "Lago di S. Daniele" (annesso all'omonimo monastero) e costruito l'Arsenale Nuovo (la Darsena Nuova), raggiungendo così un'estensione di 138.600 m2 In seguito fu aggiunta la Stradal Campagna sulla quale sorsero le attuali Fonderie, le Officine dei remi, le Corderie della Tana e il Reparto artiglieria. Con la caduta di Costantinopoli (1453) e la conseguente minaccia turca nel Mediterraneo, vennero erette la monumentale Porta di Terra, che alludeva al ruolo di Venezia quale baluardo della cristianità, e le due torri che affiancano la porta ad acqua, poi ricostruite nel Seicento. Il portale d'ingresso di terra fu costruito sulla base degli archi di trionfo romani, ed è il primo esempio di arte rinascimentale nella città. Tratto da: it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arsenale_di_Venezia

title: The Porta Magna
tags: venice italy italia venezia arsenale theportamagna
description:
The Enterance to the Arsenale.
The Porta Magna, was completed in 1460 and was the first Classical revival structure built in Venice. It was perhaps built by Antonio Gambello from a design by Jacopo Bellini. Two lions taken from Greece situated beside it were added in 1687. One of the lions, known as the Piraeus Lion, is notable for the runic defacements carved in it by invading Scandinavian mercenaries during the eleventh century.
comments:
James J Dunn: If you give a look at Venice history, you will see that the city was in the past a great naval and commercial force thanks to its strategical position at the head of the Adriatic sea. Venice was founded in the V° century A.D. by a group of refugees from northern Italy, an area of the country constantly plagued by wars and invasions. ... The refugees settled in an island and turned it into the city of Venice. They started to build houses of wattle and daub, and anchor branches with stakes to protect the pilings from the action of the currents. They started to construct boats and to obtain food thanks to the fishing activity.... They also dried sea water to extract salt and suddenly the economy of Venice was started. By the year 548 A.D. Venice was already a power controlling the full trade on the Adriatic Sea. By the IX° century, Venice developed into a city state, one of the four famous "Maritime Republics ".
They were the main commercial traders between Western Europe and the rest of the world. By the year 1104, the Porta Magna or Venice Arsenal as we know it was born. After the Fourth Crusade, Venice became an imperial power that, with 36,000 sailors  3,300 ships, controlled the commerce on Mediterranean sea....At the end of the XVII° century, Venice began to lose its commercial power because of the growing power of Portugal and by the half of XVIII° century it was already a major agricultural exporter and became later an important manufacturer centre... The current economy of Venice is based mainly on tourism. The city's beautiful architectural style, canals and other cultural and artistic heritage such as the Venetian Carnival and numerous film festivals, attract tourism from all over the world throughout the year.... The island of Murano is famous for the production of a special fine glass well appreciated and exported all around the world. Another important Venetian craft product is lace, produced on the island of Burano... The mainland - especially the towns of Mestre and Marghera - is an important shipbuilding zone with several industrial facilities such as steel processing, foundries and chemical factories.

comments: richardr: The book held open by the lion of St Mark normally reads 'Pax Tibi Marce Evangelista Meus.' However as there was little Pax to a naval dockyard, it was omitted here. The Venetian Arsenal is a shipyard and naval depot that played a leading role in Venetian empire-building. It was one of the most important areas of Venice, lying in the Castello sestiere. The Byzantine-style establishment may have existed as early as the 8th century, though the present structure is usually said to have been begun in 1104, although there is no evidence for such a precise date. It definitely existed by the early thirteenth century and is mentioned in Dante's Inferno. The name probably comes from Arabic Dar al Sina’a ("Dockyard") and the concept was clearly Islamic as much as Byzantine. Initially the state dockyard worked merely to maintain naval ships built privately, but in 1320 the Arsenal Nuovo was built, much larger than the original. It enabled all the state's navy and the larger merchant ships to be both constructed and maintained in one place. The Arsenal incidentally became an important centre for rope manufacture, while housing for the arsenal workers grew up outside its walls. Venice developed methods of mass-producing warships in the Arsenal, including the frame-first system to replace the Roman hull-first practice. The new system was much faster and required less wood. At the peak of its efficiency in the early 16th century, the Arsenal employed some 16,000 people who apparently were able to produce nearly one ship each day, and could fit out, arm, and provision a newly-built galley with standardized parts on a production-line basis not seen again until the Industrial Revolution. The staff of the Arsenal also developed new firearms at an early date, beginning with bombards in the 1370s and numerous small arms against the Genoese a few years later. Improvements in handguns led to their muzzle velocity (and therefore their ability to penetrate armor) exceeding that of the crossbow. The Venetian condottieri leader, Bartolomeo Colleoni, is usually given credit as being the first to mount the Arsenal's new lighter-weight artillery on mobile carriages for field use. The Arsenal's main gate, the Porta Magna, was built in about 1460 and was the first Classical revival structure to be built in Venice. It was perhaps built by Antonio Gambello from a design by Jacopo Bellini. The Arsenal Novissimo was begun in 1473. It enabled the creation of a system similar to an assembly line, in which hulls were constructed in the newer areas of the Arsenal before being fitted out in the old Arsenal. In the late 16th century, the Arsenal's designers experimented with larger ships as platforms for heavy naval guns. The most impressive was the galleass, already used at Lepanto, and developed from the old merchanting "great galley". It was huge, with sails as well as oars, and was virtually a floating fortress, with guns mounted on wheeled carriages along the sides in the modern fashion. It was slow and unwieldy in battle, however, and few were ever built. The galleon, also developed at the Arsenal, was an armed sailing ship, a slimmer version of the merchant "round ship". It was useful in major naval battles, but not in the small bays and off the frequent lee shores of the Dalmatian coast. Significant parts of the Arsenal were destroyed under Napoleonic rule, and later rebuilt to enable the Arsenal's present use as a naval base. It is also used as a research centre, an exhibition venue during the Venice Biennale and is home to a historic boat preservation centre. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venetian_Arsenal | FotosdeManuela: Beautiful capture | richardr: Thanks. | ribizlifozelek: This photo is considered to be an outstanding architecture shot and invited to The Pritzker Architecture Prize on flickr rating group, where you can rate other shots and your shot will be rated too. title: The Piraeus Lion
tags: old city venice urban italy sculpture building heritage history statue architecture geotagged greek italian ancient europe italia european lion historic venetian statuary venezia castello arsenal europeanunion arsenale piraeus dockyard athenian ancientgreek serenissima veneziani portoleone piraeuslion varangian arsenaledivenezia venetianarsenal lionport geo:lat=4543475 geo:lon=12350001 campoarsenale fondamentadellarsenale
comments: richardr: The Piraeus Lion is one of four lion statues on display at the Venetian Arsenal, where it was displayed as a symbol of Venice's patron saint, Saint Mark. It was originally located in Piraeus, the ancient harbour of Athens. It was seized by Doge Francesco Morosini in 1687 as plunder taken in the Great Turkish War against the Ottoman Empire, during which the Venetians besieged Athens. The lion was a famous landmark in Piraeus, having stood there since the first or second century AD. Its prominence was such that the port was given the name Porto Leone ("Lion Port") by the Italians. It is depicted in a sitting pose, with a hollow throat and the mark of a pipe (now lost) running down its back; this suggests that it was originally used as a fountain. The statue, which is made of white marble is particularly noteworthy for having been defaced some time in the second half of the 11th century by Scandinavians who carved two lengthy runic inscriptions into the shoulders and flanks of the lion. The runes are carved in the shape of an elaborate lindworm dragon-headed scroll, in much the same style as on rune stones in Scandinavia. The carvers of the runes were almost certainly Varangians, Scandinavian mercenaries in the service of the Byzantine Emperor who had been sent to Greece to put down a revolt by the local people. The inscriptions were not recognised as runes until the Swedish diplomat Johan David Åkerblad identified them at the end of the 18th century. They are in the shape of a lindworm (a flightless dragon with serpentine body and two or no legs) and were first translated in the mid-19th century by Carl Christian Rafn, the Secretary of the Kongelige Nordiske Oldskrift-Selskab (Royal Society of Nordic Antiquaries). The inscriptions are heavily eroded due to weathering and air pollution, making many of the individual runes barely legible. This has required translators to reconstruct some of the runes, filling in the blanks to determine what words they represented. The two inscriptions and Rafn's translations are as follows: Right side of the lion: Asmund cut these runes with Asgeir and Thorleif, Thord and Ivar, at the request of Harold the Tall, though the Greeks considered about and forbade it. Left side of the lion: Hakon with Ulf and Asmund conquered this port. These men and Harold Hafi imposed a heavy fine on account of the revolt of the Greek people. Dalk is detained captive in far lands. Egil is gone on an expedition with Ragnar into Romania and Armenia. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piraeus_Lion | suzigun: Lovely shot - great angle -- Seen in my contacts' photos.
